findings.finding: S3's (94f9) file_impact metadata lists 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py' and 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py' as edit targets, directly contradicting the story description body which states 'No src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py change and no new rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py — the real config home is the app-level src/rebar/_config_schema.py.' An implementer following the file_impact would edit the wrong files.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the two wrong file_impact entries with: {path: 'src/rebar/_config_schema.py', reason: 'Add backend field to ReconcilerConfig + _as_choice coercer in reconciler coercer table'}.

findings.finding: S3's file_impact list includes 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py' (reason: 'Add reconciler.backend key, default jira') and 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py' (reason: 'Static-frozenset validation of backend key') — but the S3 description body explicitly states: 'No src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py change and no new rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py — the earlier draft named both in error.' The contradictory file_impact entries will cause an implementer following the file list to open and edit the wrong files.
findings.location: S3 (94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75) file_impact list vs. description body
findings.suggested_fix: Replace S3 file_impact entries: remove `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py` and `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py`; add `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` with reason 'Add backend field to ReconcilerConfig + _as_choice coercer in reconciler coercer table'.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify ownership of IdentityConvention rewiring for dispatch_one.py:320, binding_store.py:516, and binding_walk.py:341 — either add them to S4's confirmed leak sites and Scope, or explicitly acknowledge (with rationale) that they are deferred to epic be74 and remove S2's forward commitment to S4.
evidence: S2 Approach: 'the four core call sites are NOT rewired to it here — routing them through IdentityConvention is S4 [rebar:6673-7636-a116-4f90].'
S4 confirmed leak sites list: dispatch_one.py, binding_store.py, and binding_walk.py do not appear.
S4 Scope: 'Replace the apply_inbound_records.py:70,74 "jira" provider literals with RemoteRef/IdentityConvention' — only one of the four sites S2 names.
No other sibling (S3, S5, S1) claims these three sites.
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: S2 (ce4d) explicitly commits that routing the four IdentityConvention call sites (dispatch_one.py:320, binding_store.py:516, apply_inbound_records.py:223, binding_walk.py:341) through IdentityConvention is S4's responsibility. S4 (6673) claims only apply_inbound_records.py:70,74 in its confirmed leak sites and Scope; the other three sites (dispatch_one.py:320, binding_store.py:516, binding_walk.py:341) appear in no sibling's scope, creating an owned-by-none gap.
findings.location: child ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798, Approach section ('routing them through IdentityConvention is S4'); child 6673-7636-a116-4f90, Scope section (omits dispatch_one.py, binding_store.py, binding_walk.py)
scenarios: S4 lands with dispatch_one.py:320 still using the hand-inlined 'rebar-id:{local_id}' format string. The IdentityConvention object exists (from S2) but three of its four intended call sites remain unrouted, leaving the format partially single-sourced. A future backend with different label conventions still requires core edits at those three sites, partially defeating the seam.
A reviewer checking S4's completeness against S2's forward commitment finds the three sites unclaimed and flags the story incomplete.
findings.suggested_fix: In S4, add dispatch_one.py:320, binding_store.py:516, and binding_walk.py:341 to the 'confirmed leak sites' list with the note that they are label-format inlining sites (not vendor imports) and route them through backend.identity_convention.format_label() / parse_label() in the same change. Alternatively, if intentionally deferred, remove S2's sentence 'routing them through IdentityConvention is S4' and replace with 'routing them is deferred to epic be74.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to S4's scope: migrate the test-side re-export consumers of `outbound_differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` (1 unit test) and `outbound_differ._diff_fields` (3 integration-test call sites) to reference `adapters.jira.outbound_fields` directly (or the `OutboundMapper` method), so removing `outbound_differ.py`'s `# noqa: F401` re-export import doesn't break the suite.
evidence: outbound_differ.py:63: `from rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields import (  # noqa: F401` with comment 'so outbound_differ.<name> keeps resolving for the field-diff test suite'
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85: `assert 'session_log' not in outbound_differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`
tests/integration/test_reconcile_live_e2e.py:531,561,698: `outbound_differ._diff_fields(...)`
S4 AC: 'None of the 12 rewired core modules import adapters.jira.* or the root vendor modules' — this requires removing the outbound_fields import from outbound_differ.py
S4 Scope: 'Changes callers, not file locations' — no mention of migrating test-side re-export consumers
findings.finding: S4's neutrality goal requires removing outbound_differ.py's `from rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields import _LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE, _assignee_matches, _diff_fields, _extract_jira_field, _map_local_to_jira_fields` (line :63, marked `# noqa: F401` with an explicit comment that the re-exports exist 'so outbound_differ.<name> keeps resolving for the field-diff test suite'). At least one unit test (`test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85`: `outbound_differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`) and three integration-test call sites (`test_reconcile_live_e2e.py:531,561,698`: `outbound_differ._diff_fields`) depend on this re-export contract. S4's scope says 'Changes callers, not file locations' and its AC asserts the neutrality grep returns zero hits — but removing the re-export import silently breaks these test callers. The plan provides no instruction for migrating them.
scenarios: S4 lands, outbound_differ.py's outbound_fields import is removed to satisfy the neutrality gate, and `test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py` immediately fails with AttributeError on `outbound_differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`.
Integration tests calling `outbound_differ._diff_fields` fail with AttributeError, breaking the 'full suite green at every landed change' AC.
findings.suggested_fix: In S4, when `outbound_differ.py`'s `from rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields import ...` is removed, update `test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85` to assert against `adapters.jira.outbound_fields._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` (or the Jira adapter's constant), and update `test_reconcile_live_e2e.py:531,561,698` to call `outbound_fields._diff_fields` via the adapter path. Add these files to S4's `file_impact` list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in S3 whether `reconciler.backend` validation is derived dynamically from the registry (preferred, to honor the 'only add adapters/<x>/' guarantee) or is a static enumeration — if static, document explicitly that adding a backend requires updating the validator as a second touch-point.
evidence: 'static-key validated' language implies a hardcoded Literal/enum rather than registry-derived validation
The final AC states: 'adding a backend requires only a new adapters/<x>/ implementing the pinned interface — verified by the proof-of-seam FakeBackend'
If key validation is static, a new backend author must also touch the validator, violating the single-touch-point promise
findings.finding: S3 specifies 'static-key validated' for config.reconciler.backend, which implies the set of valid backend keys is a hardcoded enumeration rather than dynamically derived from the in-tree registry. If so, adding a second backend (epic be74) requires updating both the registry AND the validator — a two-place change that undermines the plan's stated promise that 'adding a backend requires only a new adapters/<x>/ implementing the pinned interface.'
scenarios: Epic be74 author adds adapters/github/ and registers it, but the config validator still rejects 'github' because the static key list was not updated — a silent runtime failure or a confusing validation error that contradicts the 'only add adapters/<x>/' guarantee.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the config validator derive its accepted key set from the registry at validation time (e.g., `Literal[tuple(registry.keys())]` built dynamically, or a runtime check against registered keys), so registering a backend in adapters/<x>/__init__.py is the sole required change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to S3's scope an explicit documentation update (config schema reference, README section, or ADR 0035 annotation) covering `reconciler.backend`, its valid values, and the default.
evidence: 'add reconciler.backend config (static-key validated)'
'the default ("jira") reproduces today's behavior end-to-end'
ABSENCE: no mention of updating config schema docs, README, or any operator-facing reference for the new key
findings.finding: The new config key `reconciler.backend` introduced in S3 has no associated documentation update called out (config schema, README, or operator guide), leaving operators unaware of the new knob and its valid values.
scenarios: An operator upgrading rebar sees an undocumented config key in a colleague's config file and has no reference for valid values or behavior.
Auto-generated config schema docs (if they exist) go stale because no doc-update task is included in S3's scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC to S4 (6673): 'Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/**/*.py stays within the 800-LOC cap after this story lands — verified by pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py, exit 0.'
evidence: Parent AC5: 'Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py stays within the 800-LOC module-size cap (`.github/module-size-limit.txt`) throughout.'
S3 (94f9) AC: 'Every src/rebar/**/*.py stays within the 800-LOC cap — verified by pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py, exit 0.'
S5 (dfb9) AC: 'Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py stays within the 800-LOC module-size cap after the move — verified by the module-size gate.'
S4 Acceptance Criteria list contains no analogous LOC cap check item, despite S4 adding logic to adapters/jira/ (ADF-sizing, lossy-status/parent, value-maps, link-constants).
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: S4 (6673) contains no explicit Acceptance Criterion or test gate verifying the 800-LOC module-size cap, even though S4 is the largest edit (routing 12 core sites and relocating Jira-specific logic into adapters/jira/ files that may grow substantially). The parent plan requires the cap be enforced 'throughout' (at every landed change).
findings.location: child 6673-7636-a116-4f90, Acceptance Criteria section (no LOC-cap AC present)
scenarios: S4 expands adapters/jira/backend.py or another adapter file beyond 800 LOC; no gate catches it before merge; the module-size contract is broken mid-epic rather than at S5.
ADF-sizing or value-map logic relocated into adapters/jira/ causes an existing file to exceed the cap silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Add as the final AC in S4: '[ ] Every src/rebar/**/*.py stays within the 800-LOC module-size cap — verified by `pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py`, exit 0.' mirroring S3's exact formulation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC to S2 (ce4d): 'Every newly introduced src/rebar/**/*.py file stays within the 800-LOC cap — verified by pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py, exit 0.'
evidence: Parent AC5: 'Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py stays within the 800-LOC module-size cap (`.github/module-size-limit.txt`) throughout.'
S2 introduces at minimum three new source files; _backend.py aggregates five role Protocols plus three capability Protocols plus RemoteRef and could be large.
S3 and S5 both include explicit 800-LOC AC items; S2 does not.
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: S2 (ce4d) contains no explicit Acceptance Criterion verifying the 800-LOC module-size cap, even though it introduces new files (_backend.py with five role Protocols + three capability Protocols + RemoteRef, adapters/jira/backend.py, adapters/jira/identity.py). The parent plan requires the cap 'throughout'.
findings.location: child ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798, Acceptance Criteria section (no LOC-cap AC present)
scenarios: _backend.py is written as a single module consolidating all Protocol definitions and exceeds 800 LOC; no gate in S2's AC catches this before the story is accepted.
findings.suggested_fix: Add as the final AC in S2: '[ ] Every src/rebar/**/*.py stays within the 800-LOC module-size cap — verified by `pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py`, exit 0.'

evidence: Session log ticket graph: 'relates_to -> 4c1f-7409-6f2d-4010'
Plan text contains zero occurrences of '4c1f'
findings.finding: The 'relates_to -> 4c1f-7409-6f2d-4010' link present in the session log's ticket graph is silently absent from the plan — no mention of this relationship, no rationale for omitting it.
scenarios: A consumer of this epic who needs to understand cross-cutting concerns or shared context with ticket 4c1f has no traceability from the plan.
If 4c1f imposes a constraint on this epic (e.g., a shared interface or a migration dependency), implementers will miss it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the neutrality gate artifact: state in the S4 AC (and reference from the S3/S5 ACs) exactly which automated mechanism enforces import-boundary neutrality (e.g., 'a `pytest` `test_neutrality.py` that asserts no `rebar_reconciler/*.py` imports `adapters.jira.*` or the legacy vendor root modules, run as part of `make test`').
evidence: 'enforced by a neutrality gate on the **12 rewired core sites**' — no artifact type, file path, or command specified
Absence: no mention of a lint plugin, pytest conftest boundary check, or CI script that implements the gate
Without the artifact type an executor must 'choose an appropriate' enforcement mechanism — a classic E2 placeholder signal
findings.finding: The 'neutrality gate on the 12 rewired core sites' is named as the enforcement mechanism in the S4 AC but its implementation form is left entirely to the executor — the plan does not state whether this is a CI lint rule, a pytest import-boundary assertion, a custom script, or a manual review step. This is a 'choose an appropriate X' deferral. Additionally, without specifying the artifact, the criterion is not durably evaluable in-session (F1): if the gate is only a manual claim, it cannot be re-run to stay true.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S4 bullet: 'enforced by a neutrality gate on the 12 rewired core sites'
scenarios: An executor implements a manual grep in a PR description instead of an automated check; the gate is not durable and future PRs can silently regress neutrality.
Different executors choose different mechanisms (lint vs. test vs. script), making the criterion unevenly enforced across the epic's stories.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the S4 AC: 'Neutrality is enforced by `tests/engine/test_import_boundaries.py::test_core_neutrality`, which asserts that none of the 12 rewired modules import `adapters.jira.*` or the legacy vendor-root modules, and this test is added in S4 and remains green through S5.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either enumerate the opt-in capability Protocols by name and assign each to a story (S2 is the natural home), or explicitly state 'opt-in capability Protocols are out of scope for Phase 2 and tracked separately' in the Design section and AC.
evidence: 'Five role Protocols (TicketTransport, OutboundMapper, InboundMapper, FieldSanitizer, IdentityConvention) behind one Backend facade, + opt-in capability Protocols.'
Absence: no capability Protocol names, no story assignment (S1–S5), no AC line referencing them
The AC for S2 says only 'the Backend Protocols, RemoteRef, and a contract suite … exist' — it is ambiguous whether 'Backend Protocols' includes capability Protocols
findings.finding: The Design section introduces 'opt-in capability Protocols' as a first-class architectural element alongside the five named role Protocols, but neither the AC nor the child-story decomposition assigns them to a story, specifies which capabilities exist, or states whether they are required for this epic or deferred. An executor cannot determine if implementing them is in scope for Phase 2.
findings.location: Design section — 'Five role Protocols … + opt-in capability Protocols'
scenarios: An executor implements zero capability Protocols assuming they are deferred; another implements several, causing scope creep and S2 story-size explosion.
Epic be74 assumes a specific capability Protocol exists but it was not built in Phase 2, blocking the second-backend epic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the failure mode for unknown backend keys in the S3 spec: e.g., 'raises `ConfigurationError` with message \'Unknown backend: {key}\' at config-load time (eager validation)'.
evidence: 'add reconciler.backend config (static-key validated)' — validation timing and error type not stated
Absence: no mention of ValueError vs. ConfigurationError, no mention of whether the check is eager (startup) or lazy (first call to select_backend())
findings.finding: The S3 AC states the registry performs 'static-key validated' lookup but does not specify the failure mode when an unrecognised backend key is supplied — error type, message format, and whether validation happens at config-load time or at first use. This is a defaultable gap an executor must silently resolve.
findings.location: Child decomposition S3 and Acceptance Criteria — S3 bullet: 'static-key validated'
scenarios: Executor implements lazy validation; a misconfigured backend key silently falls through until the first reconciliation run, making misconfiguration hard to detect.
Two executors choose different error types, creating inconsistency with rebar's existing config-validation conventions.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S3 description: 'Unknown keys raise `ConfigurationError(f"Unknown reconciler backend: {key!r}; registered: {list(registry)}")` at config-load time, consistent with rebar's existing config validation pattern.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit step to the S5 decomposition body describing the repointing of `inbound_fields.py` and `outbound_comments.py` to their post-relocation import paths, so the S5 story body fully covers all work verified by AC bullet 4.
evidence: S5 body: 'physically relocate acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits into adapters/jira/, teach the file-location loader the new dir, and migrate the ~29 test patch strings atomically (no shims)' — no mention of repointing sibling-referencing modules
AC bullet 4: 'the two non-moved root modules that reference them by sibling filename (inbound_fields.py, outbound_comments.py) are repointed' — appears only in AC, absent from S5 body
findings.finding: The S5 story description lists modules to relocate (acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits) and actions to take (teach the loader, migrate ~29 test patch strings) but does NOT mention repointing `inbound_fields.py` and `outbound_comments.py`. The S5 AC bullet adds this as a required deliverable ('the two non-moved root modules that reference them by sibling filename (inbound_fields.py, outbound_comments.py) are repointed'). This creates an orphan AC requirement with no backing decomposition description — an implementer reading only the S5 story body would miss this work.
findings.location: S5 decomposition paragraph + S5 AC bullet 4
scenarios: Implementer completes S5 scope as described, ships the PR, passes CI, but inbound_fields.py and outbound_comments.py still reference the old sibling paths — runtime breakage discovered only after merge.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the S5 story paragraph: 'Also repoint the two non-relocated root modules (`inbound_fields.py`, `outbound_comments.py`) that currently import their siblings by relative/filename reference to use the new `adapters/jira/` paths.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the S5 AC bullet to explicitly assert removal of the originals from the package root (e.g., 'the pre-migration root-level files are deleted and are no longer importable from their old paths') so the criterion verifies both halves of the migration.
evidence: AC bullet 4: 'acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits reside under adapters/jira/, the loader resolves the location-pinned modules there … and all ~29 test patch strings are migrated with no re-export shims'
ABSENCE: no clause asserting that the files no longer exist at the package root or are not importable from the old location
findings.finding: The S5 AC bullet verifies the replacement side of the file migration ('acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits reside under adapters/jira/') but does not verify REMOVAL — i.e. it does not assert that the originals are deleted from the package root and are no longer importable there. For a migration AC, both removal and replacement should be verified; the current criterion would be satisfied by copying files rather than moving them.
scenarios: A copy-not-move implementation satisfies the AC as written; the old root-level modules remain importable, allowing new code to accidentally import from the old location and defeating the seam entirely.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC bullet 4: '… and the pre-move root-level modules (`acli*`, `adf.py`, `outbound_fields.py`, `comment_limits.py`) are deleted from the package root (no importable copy remains at the old path).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'neutrality gate' concretely in the S4 description (or a referenced ADR section): specify whether it is a pytest import-boundary test, a CI linting rule, a banned-import configuration, or another artifact, and where it lives in the repository.
evidence: AC bullet 3: 'enforced by a neutrality gate on the 12 rewired core sites'
S4 body: 'route every core consumer through the Backend port' — describes routing, never mentions what a 'neutrality gate' is or where it lives
Design section: no mention of 'neutrality gate'
ABSENCE: no plan section defines or describes the 'neutrality gate' artifact
findings.finding: The S4 AC bullet introduces the term 'neutrality gate' as the enforcement mechanism ('enforced by a neutrality gate on the 12 rewired core sites') but no section of the plan — neither the S4 story description, the Design section, nor the Approach — defines what artifact a 'neutrality gate' is (a CI linting rule, a pytest import-assertion, a flake8 plugin, a custom script, etc.). Without a definition, the criterion is unverifiable: two implementers could produce completely different enforcement artifacts and both claim compliance.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S4 bullet 3; S4 decomposition paragraph
scenarios: One implementer writes a pytest conftest import-boundary assertion; another adds a flake8 banned-import rule; a third manually confirms via grep and documents nothing. All three claim to satisfy the AC, and none can objectively verify the others.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a clause to the S3 AC bullet (or a dedicated sub-bullet) verifying that an unrecognized `config.reconciler.backend` value is rejected at config-load time with a clear error, matching the 'static-key validated' requirement in the S3 description.
evidence: S3 body: 'add reconciler.backend config (static-key validated)'

AC bullet 2: 'The backend is chosen by config.reconciler.backend via the registry; the default ("jira") reproduces today's behavior end-to-end' — verifies selection and default, not validation of invalid keys
ABSENCE: no AC clause requiring that an invalid backend key is rejected at config-load time
findings.finding: The S3 story description specifies 'static-key validated' as a property of the `config.reconciler.backend` config key, but no AC bullet verifies this validation behavior. The described deliverable (config validation rejects unknown backend keys) has no covering criterion — an implementation that accepts any string and only fails at runtime when `select_backend()` is called would satisfy all AC bullets as written.
findings.location: S3 decomposition paragraph + Acceptance Criteria — S3 bullet 2
scenarios: A misconfigured deployment specifying 'githubissues' (a typo) silently falls through to a runtime KeyError or AttributeError at reconciliation time rather than a clear config-validation error — the regression that static-key validation is meant to prevent.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC bullet 2: '… and an unrecognized backend key raises a descriptive `ConfigError` at load time (static-key validation), not a runtime exception during reconciliation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out plan for S5's destructive no-shim relocation (e.g., a named git-revert strategy, a feature-flag gate, or a two-step expand-then-contract sequence) so recovery from a failed S5 land is documented.
evidence: 'physically relocate acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits into adapters/jira/'
'no re-export shims'
'migrate the ~29 test patch strings atomically'
ABSENCE: no rollback plan, no expand-contract sequence, no back-out step described anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: S5 performs a destructive, no-shim file relocation of acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits with no stated back-out or rollback path; the plan requires reversibility mechanism, not just a 'behavior-preserving' assertion.
findings.location: Child decomposition S5 / AC bullet 4 ('no re-export shims')
scenarios: S5 lands and a missed patch string or loader regression causes suite failure in CI; team must revert a large atomic commit with no documented recovery procedure.
A downstream tool or script outside the test suite imports from the old path; breakage is silent until runtime because no shim catches it.
findings.suggested_fix: Insert a 'Rollback / back-out' subsection under S5: 'If the post-move suite is not fully green, revert the S5 commit atomically (single commit boundary); the prior S4 state is self-consistent and deployable. No partial rollback is needed because S5 edits only file locations + patch strings, not behavior.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] S4's Testing section should specify that new tests calling select_backend() use _reset_registry_for_test() (from S3) or an equivalent fixture to guarantee a clean registry state, and that the JiraBackend is explicitly re-registered within each such test's setup.
evidence: S4 Testing: 'For run_differs, introduce a new seam... add a fresh test that patches the new seam (there is no existing patch target to reuse).'
S4 Testing: '_attestation.py: route build_acli_client_from_env through the backend transport... covered by a dedicated test that stubs the backend selection.'
S4 makes no reference to _reset_registry_for_test() in its Testing or Approach sections.

S3 Testing: '_reset_registry_for_test round-trips registry state — verified by pytest ... -k "idempotent or reset"' and 'the reset helper lets the teeth test register a FakeBackend and tear it down without leaking process state.'
The registry is module-level state; tests that call select_backend() without resetting it depend on registration side-effects from import order.
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: S4 (6673) introduces new tests for the run_differs.py and _attestation.py seams that call select_backend() — registry-dependent calls — but S4's Testing section makes no mention of using S3's _reset_registry_for_test() context manager. Without resetting registry state, these new S4 tests may depend on implicit module-level registry population order, causing test-isolation failures when run in different orderings or in isolation.
findings.location: child 6673-7636-a116-4f90, Testing section ('add a fresh test that patches the new seam'); child 94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75, Testing section ('_reset_registry_for_test round-trips')
scenarios: S4's new run_differs or _attestation test is run in isolation (pytest path filter) before adapters/__init__.py has been imported; the registry is empty; select_backend() raises a registry-error rather than returning JiraBackend; the test fails for the wrong reason.
A test that registers a FakeBackend in a S4 test fixture leaks into the run_differs seam test, returning the wrong backend.
findings.suggested_fix: In S4's Testing section, add: 'Each new test that invokes select_backend() wraps its body in _reset_registry_for_test() to guarantee a clean registry, then re-registers JiraBackend before asserting, mirroring S3's teeth-test pattern.'

evidence: Session log title: 'TDD held-out, stacked Gerrit landing'

Plan AC mentions 'contract suite passing against both JiraBackend and a test-only FakeBackend' but does not specify that the contract suite must be authored (and committed) before or independently of the implementation it validates.
No story in S1–S5 calls out a held-out test commit as a separate deliverable or ordering constraint.
findings.finding: The session log title explicitly names 'TDD held-out' as a key methodology constraint for this epic, but the plan contains no mention of held-out tests, test-first ordering within stories, or any AC enforcing that contract/acceptance tests are committed before the implementation code they exercise.
scenarios: A developer treating S2 as 'write interface + JiraBackend + contract tests together in one commit' satisfies the plan's AC but violates the TDD held-out methodology, producing no held-out test evidence.
If the held-out test requirement is a process gate (e.g., Gerrit review checks for a test-only patch before an implementation patch), its absence from the plan means the gate is never configured.
